title: Filmer and His Critics
author: Johann Sommerville
notes: Essential secondary source on Filmer / Patriarcha

title: Kings and Kinship in Early Israel
author: Tomoo Ishida
notes: OT scholarship on Israelite monarchy; the institution in ancient context

title: Kingship and the Gods
author: Henri Frankfort
notes: Divine kingship across the ancient Near East; comparative context for OT monarchy; the king as mediator between divine and human realms

title: The King's Two Bodies
author: Ernst Kantorowicz
notes: Foundational medieval political theology; the king's natural and mystical body; essential for understanding how oaths bind to the office not the person

title: The Divine Right of Kings
author: J.N. Figgis
notes: Classic historical survey of divine right theory from medieval origins through James I; fair-minded and thorough

title: Political Thought in Medieval Times
author: John B. Morrall
notes: Good overview of medieval monarchy theory including oath/fealty structures

title: Covenant and Polity in Biblical Israel
author: Daniel Elazar
notes: Covenantal political theory from OT; relevant to oath/covenant structures and how they differ from contract

title: Law and Revolution
author: Harold Berman
notes: The Gregorian reform and its effect on oath/fealty legal structures; how canon law shapes political theology

title: Liberty or Equality
author: Erik von Kuehnelt-Leddihn
notes: Catholic monarchist argument; monarchy vs. democracy as political theology; argues democracy is inherently leveling and tyrannical

title: Democracy: The God That Failed
author: Hans-Hermann Hoppe
notes: Secular (anarcho-capitalist) argument that monarchy is more stable and less destructive than democracy; useful for the natural order argument without theological premises; pairs well with Yarvin

title: Yale Open Courses — Old Testament lectures (Christine Hayes)
author: Yale University
notes: Historical-critical context of Israelite monarchy; useful background before engaging the theological arguments

title: GotQuestions.org — What is the Divine Right of Kings?
author: GotQuestions staff
notes: Introductory synthesis tying OT texts to the divine right tradition

title: Fordham Medieval Sourcebook — Feudal Oaths
author: Fordham University
notes: Primary source collection of feudal oath documents; best single online resource for oath primary sources

title: Unqualified Reservations blog (esp. 'A Formalist Manifesto' 2007; 'Patchwork' series 2008; 'Divine-right monarchy for the modern secular intellectual' 2010)
author: Curtis Yarvin (Mencius Moldbug)
notes: Yarvin's foundational neoreactionary corpus. Key arguments: democracy is a disguised oligarchy run by 'the Cathedral' (universities/media/NGOs); monarchy = formal single sovereign = clear accountability; the 'Patchwork' vision of sovereign city-states as joint-stock corporations; formalism (align formal power with real power). Most relevant for your thesis: his secular re-articulation of the natural-order-of-monarchy argument; parallels Deut 17 in spirit (regulated single ruler) without divine anointing. Note: Yarvin is self-consciously secular and post-Christian — his framework needs theological supplementation for your purposes, not replacement of your own

title: Gray Mirror Substack (esp. 'We Don't Have to Live Like This' 2021; 'Monarchism and Fascism Today'; 'A Conversation About Monarchy')
author: Curtis Yarvin
notes: Later Yarvin; refines the monarchy/fascism distinction (monarchy: calm autocratic neutral; fascism: mass-mobilizing populist); practical pathways including RAGE (Retire All Government Employees) and Caesarist transition models; some posts paywalled

title: The Anatomy of Fascism
author: Robert Paxton
notes: Useful counterpoint: rigorous definition of fascism vs. monarchy; helps sharpen the Yarvin distinction and avoid conflation in your own argument

title: The Origins of Political Order
author: Francis Fukuyama
notes: Fukuyama traces the development of political institutions across civilizations; monarchy as the default pre-modern order; useful empirical background for the 'monarchy is natural' thesis — even from a non-monarchist scholar

title: Political Order and Political Decay
author: Francis Fukuyama
notes: Sequel; the decay of democratic institutions; inadvertently strengthens the case that democratic forms are unstable

title: Leviathan
author: Thomas Hobbes
notes: Hobbes is not a monarchist in the traditional sense but his argument for absolute sovereign power — grounded in human nature and the war of all against all — provides a secular philosophical foundation for undivided rule that Yarvin explicitly updates; the oath/covenant to the sovereign in Hobbes is foundational
